name: Reliable Data Transfer Mechanisms
description: Quiz on Pipelining, Go-Back-N (GBN) and Selective Repeat (SR).

questions.question: What fundamentally characterizes the behavior of pipelined protocols in a network?
options: The sender transmits multiple packets continuously without waiting for their ACKs.
The switching router consolidates all received packets to optimize the local network.
The receiver halts the receive window whenever there is high delay on the data network.
The protocol forces early encryption of the bytes aiming for a failure-free flow.

questions.question: Comparing the performance of the protocols, what is the main advantage of Selective Repeat (SR) over Go-Back-N (GBN)?
options: GBN requires fiber connections; SR works exclusively on wireless radio links.
GBN may end up retransmitting many packets for one error; SR repeats only the lost one.
GBN applies native congestion control; SR delegates that check to the local network.
GBN consumes less router memory; SR drops all packets that leave its route.

questions.question: In the Go-Back-N (GBN) protocol architecture, how does the mechanism for sending and validating acknowledgements (ACK) natively work?
options: GBN adopts individual ACKs, so that each datagram is evaluated in isolation.
GBN drops the ACK response, since it trusts the hardware timer of its local card.
GBN uses cumulative ACKs, where ACK(n) confirms the receipt of all packets up to n.
GBN sends negative ACKs (NAK) to confirm the success of flows in lossless networks.

questions.question: How does Selective Repeat (SR) react if it receives a packet out of the expected order for that ongoing connection?
options: It instantly discards everything received out of the sequence of its connection.
It delegates the reassembly of the frames to the application layer to process later.
It sends a message back to the sender requiring the reset of that interface.
It stores the out-of-order packets in a buffer until the missing packet is read.

questions.question: What is the main consequence and benefit of adopting Pipelining instead of the traditional 'stop-and-wait' model?
options: To intensely reduce the volume of the transmitted flow in order to prevent congestion.
To dramatically increase link utilization compared with the stop-and-wait model.
To natively limit the number of bits in the IP header to speed up all routers.
To prevent any fragmentation of the packets within the routers in the global web core.

questions.question: In an ideal Selective Repeat (SR) scenario, how does the sender host mechanically handle the management of its timers?
options: The SR sender groups absolutely all datagrams using just a single unified clock.
The SR sender dispenses with timers due to the natural shielding of the physical layer.
The SR sender must logically keep an individual timer for each unACKed packet.
The SR sender hands the time calculation off to the edge switch to free up CPU.

questions.question: To avoid catastrophic problems where the receiver cannot distinguish new packets from duplicates in SR, what is the golden mathematical rule?
options: The window size must be increased whenever the link shows intermittent noise.
The window size must always be exactly equal to the value of the propagation delay.
The window size completely ignores losses and grows without any mathematical limit.
The window size must be less than or equal to half of its sequence number space.

questions.question: Implementing Pipelined sending requires some hardware and architecture costs. What is one of the main requirements added to the machine?
options: The mandatory use of buffering on both the sender host and the receiver host.
The use of optical-fiber cabling to support the light speeds imposed.
The installation of cloud switches so that each packet is secretly analyzed.
Turning off the local clocks to avoid the sudden cancellation of a datagram.

questions.question: In how the Go-Back-N protocol works, what will the sender do if it suffers a timeout on one of its local timers?
options: The sender will start sending strictly only the packet that was not acknowledged.
The system will retransmit the faulty packet and also the following ones in that window.
The link will be closed by the router right after the connection time silently expires.
The receiving application automatically drops the video flow and warns all peers.

questions.question: During Selective Repeat (SR), what will the sender logically do when it successfully receives the ACK of a packet that is within its current window?
options: It ignores the content of the isolated ACK until a cumulative ACK covers the whole session.
It deletes all data held in the cache memory and dispatches an alert to the routers.
It marks that packet as received and advances the base of its window if it is the oldest.
It ends the three-way handshake, making room for the communication of other links.

questions.question: Besides buffering, what major change to the base structure of the protocols must be made to correctly support continuous sending via Pipelining?
options: The IP addressing space will need to jump from the old 32 bits to the 128-bit range.
Packets need to adopt very short cryptographic numbers so as not to overload routers.
Local transport ports must be fixed solely around the low values.
The number of possible sequence numbers needs to be increased in the transmissions.

questions.question: In the famous 'Selective Repeat Dilemma', which chronic problem occurs if the receive window is disproportionately large relative to the sequence numbers?
options: The sender immediately consumes the entire bandwidth of the Internet access provider.
The receiver becomes unable to tell a new packet of the flow apart from a retransmission.
The packet switch's memory overloads due to the failure of its output ports.
The twisted-pair cable heats up absurdly, causing short circuits across the whole network.

questions.question: Within network architecture, at which layer do Pipelining mechanisms (such as Selective Repeat and Go-Back-N) operate, and what is their main objective?
options: They operate at the Transport Layer to guarantee logical communication between processes.
They run at the Network Layer to ensure global routing between hosts.
They operate at the Link Layer to prevent physical bit collisions on the access cable.
They run at the Application Layer to define the exact semantics and message types.
